Name: spectral_precision_routing
Title: Spectral Precision Routing for Efficient and Adaptive Inference
Short Hypothesis: By leveraging spectral quantization theory and low-overhead implementation, we can develop a curvature-aware precision routing framework that dynamically allocates numerical precision based on real-time activation geometry, leading to significant improvements in inference efficiency and accuracy.
Related Work: Our proposal is closely related to recent works such as Spectral Quantization Routing (SQR) and Spectral Latent Steering (SLS), which have demonstrated the potential of spectral methods for efficient inference. However, our approach differs in its focus on curvature-aware precision routing and its integration with low-overhead implementation, which enables more efficient and adaptive inference. Additionally, our work draws inspiration from Geometric Signal Separation and Training-Free Geometric Interference Cancellation, which have shown the effectiveness of geometric methods for robust in-context learning.
Abstract: We propose a novel spectral precision routing framework that leverages curvature-aware precision allocation and low-overhead implementation to achieve efficient and adaptive inference. Our approach dynamically allocates numerical precision based on real-time activation geometry, using spectral quantization theory to identify the most critical components of the input data. We evaluate our framework on single-GPU setups across various benchmarks, demonstrating significant improvements in throughput-accuracy Pareto frontiers compared to static and heuristic mixed-precision baselines. Our approach provides a transparent, reproducible pathway for resource-efficient inference under natural distribution shifts, and establishes representation geometry as a direct, mathematically transparent lever for system-scale latency optimization.
Experiments: Evaluate the proposed spectral precision routing framework on single-GPU setups across various benchmarks, including WILDS and ICL benchmarks.
Compare the performance of our approach with static and heuristic mixed-precision baselines, as well as other state-of-the-art methods such as SQR and SLS.
Investigate the effect of different spectral quantization techniques and low-overhead implementation strategies on the performance of our framework.
Analyze the trade-offs between inference efficiency, accuracy, and computational overhead in our approach, and explore methods to optimize these trade-offs.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The proposed framework may require significant computational resources and expertise to implement and optimize.
The effectiveness of our approach may depend on the specific characteristics of the input data and the chosen benchmarks.
Our framework may not be suitable for all types of machine learning models or applications, and may require additional modifications or extensions to be effective.
